top_rant.text:
Firestone Audio Spitfire Mark II

DAC reviews always seem a bit like a crap shoot to me.... At least like trying to draw broad stroke differences between two items with very minor changes in sound. However in regard to my last two DAC's the Firestone Audio people have put forth a product that I can draw some distinct and clear differences from.

The Spitfire MKII sells for anywhere from $200 to $260 USD. Right now there are a couple different vendors selling on Amazon for around $206.00 however most audio focused vendors seem to be selling them closer to the MSRP $260.00 price point. The Spitfire comes in a relatively decent package. The cardboard box is probably somewhere between retail and wholesale class. It's not exactly the type of packaging that is going to catch your eye at a retail outlet like say the ALO Audio amplifier packaging but I don't particularly care about the card board they wrap products in. I will admit that the ALO Audio boxes that my portable rigs came in serve as cord storage for my desktop system because their Marshall Amp aesthetic is nice enough to keep out on the desktop, but the fact that Firestone didn't put more into their packaging is a far cry from a deal breaker. Inside the box you'll find a warranty card, a quick start users manual, a small wall wart power supply, USB 2.0 cable and a thick though cheap looking RCA cable.

The Spitfire MKII adds a USB 2.0 input, where the first run Spitfires were limited to optical and coaxial. The USB 2.0 input has to run through a USB 2.0 plug in your laptop or computer. If you plug it into a Super-Speed or power boost USB hub... you will be met with some nasty static and feedback in your signal chain that will not be so overt that it will be obvious... It'll will scratch and crack at the edge of transients... which drives deep fear into the music lover that their new piece of kit is either crappy or exposing their itunes library for a bunch of compressed junk... Take heart, simply move the cable into a proper USB 2.0 and you're all good. The Firestone USB 2.0 does not play well with USB 3.0 so make sure you're using the correct USB hub without "SS" markings or a lightning bolt.... Its one of those things that seems obvious, but could cause some unnecessary grief for people who aren't aware of the class of their USB inputs. It's also not a cut and dry thing since I can use my little laptop stalwart Fiio DAC through the SS and powered hub without a problem.... The Spitfire, for whatever reason, is not so forgiving.

The only real knock I have on this device is that the RCA jacks on the Spitfire do not inspire confidence. They seem very fragile and should I connect cable to brusquely or without taking care, are likely to snap of whatever connectors hold them in place. I cannot say for certain that they will fail, but seem likely to fail if one were to be pulling and re-connecting RCA lines constantly. Based on the uneasy feeling I got connecting it to my desktop amp, it is not the type of DAC you want to include in a semi portable set up that you are going to be breaking down each evening to throw in your bag. My own impression is that these jacks won't hold up. Rather than tempt fate, I've opted to just buy a pair of RCA cables specifically for the Spitfire so if I need to connect or disconnect another DAC or swap amps, I just pull the wires on the other side of the chain.

Intel of the Inside

The Spitfire uses a Burr Brown PCM 2704 USB Receiver chip to a DIR9001 digital audio interface on to a Burr Brown PCM 1793 DAC chip with 24/96hz capability. The PCM 1793 is a solid DAC chip though not quite a super desirable chip in view of the audiophile elite.... However implementations matter and I dare say Firestone has gotten as much out of this 1793 as most will get out of the much higher regarded 1794. Final output buffering is done by a socketed LM4562 opamp which can be rolled to affect the sound.

The USB input is limited to 16/44 but considering I won't be running anything other than iTunes through USB it's perfectly fine for me.... It won't be upsampling my music and to me that is all good. Many place a DAC's value in the so called resolution that it is capable of and see anything lower than 24/96 as primitive. I don't see (hear) much difference between 16/44 and 24/96 myself.... but I understand that I may be in the minority here.

For the sake of stirring the pot I'll just say that most if not all of the audible spectrum (key word there is audible!) can be captured in a 16/44 playback and certainly in 24/96... the freak or two out there that can hear beyond a -8db noise floor or wants to destroy their ears with 130 db onslaughts..... or happens to be able to hear beyond normal human range of 20khz well I guess they'll have to take exception to this rig. By all means they can spend more for 24 or even 32/192hz to capture sonic frequencies only a bat or dog can make out.

I understand. The fact that such resolutions are used by and called "studio master" quality is tantalizing... a mite bit misleading on its face but tantalizing nonetheless. Ultimately if it makes someone happy, I am all for that... happiness is a good thing.

But for my purposes. 16/44 will do fine.

Build Quality

Besides the big question mark I have regarding the durability of the RCA jacks, this really is a nicely executed device. I've done some more fiddling with the RCA jacks and though I see why I may have been initially pensive about their durability.... the more I look at them the more I am inclined to believe they are up to snuff. Because the overall build is somewhat dainty, I may have been expecting a bit too much.

The Spitfire is much smaller than I had envisioned as it measures a mere 4" long x 3" wide x 2" tall, however it is still a very nice looking little box. An array of LED lights along the front face display, power, signal lock, playback status, and USB connection status.... The black anodized aluminum face is roughly 10mm thick with a small grain blasted finish and the "SPITFIRE MKII" print on the face is printed with white paint.

The sides and back are anodized aluminum with a very fine grain blasted finish and ribbing along both sides. It's not exactly saying "LOOK AT ME" like some of the more handsome components in my collection, but it is a "cute" form factor. It doesn't scream high end, but it is well done for the price point it sits in. Also if I opted to build out my computer audio set up exclusively with Firestone Audio components, they are all shipped in identically sized enclosures so you can stack amps, PSU's and DAC's into a neat 4" x 3" tower without taking up much desktop space. I actually have the separate "Supplier" power unit coming so we'll see (A) if it adds anything to the sound and (B) just how cute these little boxes look stacked.

The Sound

Mind you I don't have extensive experience with DAC's. I've used some older NOS Chinese cheapies with my Stereo setup but usually played these out of speakers. I assume my Krell dock system has an internal DAC but they are pretty tight lipped if not silent about conversion technology in the Krell Papa Dock/ Kid system.... hell they even go to the length of explaining that there is nothing of use or serviceable inside... with the understanding that there is No Reason to Open it. If it didn't cost a total of $5K I would pull out the wrenches/ screwdrivers right now, but I don't want to tempt fate.

That said after listening to the Firestone Audio DAC I don't see any need to upgrade or keep searching for my DAC. I have found it.

I did some switching from my Carver receiver, my Pioneer vintage rig my laptop staple Fiio E10 and the Firestone and the one thing that stood out with headphones (I did not test it through speakers) was the extra weight that Firestone brought to the fight. It sounds more dense, has more impact and is full bodied. I love the little Fiio E10 with iTunes using the little bass boost switch as it made a number of tracks pretty close to the tonal density I like and seemed to add some extra oomph and weight to the full spectrum of sound, but the Firestone out does it in every regard. The bass and mid range on the Firestone are strong, robust and hard hitting. But they are not overpowering even with bass heavy cans like my Denon D5000's. The bass is big and weight and thick but does not get boomy to where it starts impeding on the quality of mid range sound. I actually was so surprised when I heard it that I went scrolling through my settings on my computer to see if I had the bass enhancer on in the Sound settings or a weird EQ set up in my iTunes presets.... nope. The Firestone people have done good.

What is more is that the Spitfire is able to mine down deep into the lower frequencies to get all the meat out of the song without forgetting about the quality of the upper-mids and treble. Transients like cymbal crashes, and the constant snap of the high hat have a more analog quality... The Spitfire provides a clear and clean upper mid range and treble. Some of my "hotter" recordings are older punk albums recorded in small studios with likely some sub par gear. In the wrong setting, with the wrong cans and the wrong volume level I can hit a note or two every once in a while that will scorch my ears let alone my nerves with white hot treble.... kinda like that little knee buckling zinger you'll get every once in a while at your dentist. With the Spitfire in charge of laying everything down, there is none of the high harsh sibilance or brightness that moves beyond enjoyable.... The treble is there, but it sounds like a cymbal being hit on a drum kit not a screeching hiss coming out of my headphones to claw at my ear drums.

The treble is detailed and its is present but it has a very smoothed out quality to it, especially when I contrast it to my Fiio and older NOS Chinese Valab knock off with its 40 some odd 1543 Phillips chips (kidding).

I imagine it sounds how it did when the band played it.

I guess the best compliment I can give this little device is that it made me excited about my music library again. I haven't been spending a lot of time with my iTunes library recently. With this little device tethered to the laptop I am excited to go back through my library and enjoy tracks that I hadn't for a while. If Operation Ivy sounds this good, how is Jawbreaker going to sound?

The Spitfire isn't exactly audiophile grade device. If only because it's Chinese heritage and diminutive form factor make it a non-starter for many a stodgy audiophile. However for the more common folk like myself who are interested in getting big gains in sound quality without breaking the bank and who simply want to enjoy their music library with a set it and forget device, the Spitfire deserves consideration. It has earned big scores from big shots reviewers and in my experience, it deserves it. It will give you huge improvements over your on board computer and leaves the budget grade DAC's in the dust. It makes inherently hot iTunes music sound much more analog and live.

It's an awesome little DAC.
Try it, you'll like it.
top_rant.title: Firestone Spitfire MKII - The Little Box that could

top_rant.text:
I'm now pretty much middle-aged, so a lot of my attitude about things has changed. I've never been a particularly organized or clean person - always had more important things to concern myself with - but recently I find I'm increasingly aggravated by mess. One big source of mess in my home is my beloved cat, Desi, who while basically a very good boy in terms of personality, has taken up a very bad habit of messing on the carpet. SO, in recent times, I've been thinking more and more about cleaning my floors, and I'd been debating in my head whether to rent a carpet cleaning machine or buy one. I finally settled on buying because then I can clean on my time and my terms. I did a little bit of homework on which to get, and then settled on the Hoover MaxExtract 77. Please note, I bought mine at Wal Mart, so this review will focus on strictly my opinion of the machine and how to use it, rather than anything to do with an Amazon purchase experience. For the record, I ADORE Amazon and have had nothing but dozens of good experiences. It's just that it was cheaper to buy it at Wally World at the time, so there you go.

First off... My unit appeared to have been opened and partially assembled (one of the bad things about Wally World) and then half-a** packed back in the box. Everything looked clean, nothing broken, nothing missing, so I put it all together and gave it a try anyways. Assembly was dead easy, and I didn't even look at the manual before my first use because frankly a child could figure this one out. That's why I went through so much cleaning solution on the first go, lol. More on that later.

For my first attack on soiled carpet, I picked the worst spot in the house, where my... cough... beloved a**-cat had been urinating frequently when I wasn't home. The carpet in that small spot was a deep brown (normally gray) and dense with it. I knew this would make or break my opinion of the machine straight away, and plus that spot's been ticking me off for a long time now, and I wanted it gone. In retrospect, I should have shelved some of my other expenses and done this a LOT sooner.

I filled the cleaning solution part of the tank (it's molded together as one tank) and the water part with full hot water from the tap, then threw everything together and went at it. I didn't realize you don't hold the trigger down continuously (ooops) which keeps spraying cleaning solution into the carpet, so in one go I way oversoaked the carpet and ran through water and solution like mad bananas. Maybe it's not that bad a thing - it was a BAD spot. It was during this first mission that I learned three things:

1) The machine is heavy. You will get a good workout while cleaning your floors, and you do need to go back and forth over each strip of floor quite a lot, first to soak and scrub, and then to continue scrubbing while pulling dirty solution out of the carpet or off the flooring. Make one or two back and forth passes over the area with the trigger pressed, then back and forth a bunch of times without it pressed until you stop seeing lots of foamy water coming back into the waste tank.

2) Don't smell the waste water as you pour it outside or down your toilet, especially if you're cleaning up pet mess. O M G... there is nothing nastier on the planet, I'm sure. As much solution as I went through on that small patch of floor, it kept coming and coming like an army of psychopaths - UGLY brown. I just got done doing another larger patch of the floor, in fact, and though I thought that area was fairly clean, still very dark wastewater. This means it works and works well, obviously. Point is, expect it to gross you out, badly. Just remember, this is why you made the purchase.

3) There's really no such thing as sucking the carpeting dry, so just do the best you can and then put a fan on it. Air conditioning and/or a very low humidity day will really help move it along toward dry. If you want to really impress yourself, in this early stage, try touching it barefoot when doing carpet, and you'll be delighted at how soft it feels on your feet. My carpeting is very dense low pile, and still... that first spot felt so fluffy I was amazed.

There were two bad spots on that small patch of floor, and the basic stain didn't fully come out, but I'm not surprised - considering the severity especially of the one spot, I'd probably need a very strong, pet-specialized solution to really wipe the spot out. In general the machine did VERY well on it.

After you're done with a patch of carpet and you've run through a tankful of solution, give yourself a break and a pat on the back, empty out the wastewater tank, and if you're going to hang it up for the day, then break the machine down and clean it up and leave everything to dry. The waste tank opens up in two pieces for good rinsing, thankfully, and all you need to do with the solution tank is just rinse each of the two sides out and put all the plastic parts out on someplace to air dry. Don't forget, there's a screen filter on the machine itself, toward the handle side on the right. Make sure you clean it out well because hair and particles will clog it up eventually. Vacuuming the floor first is a NECESSITY, but still.. this machine will pick up things the vacuum won't. Again, that's why you got it.

I conclude with this:

I'm very glad I got this machine, SO FAR. They say that all such machines will break, and most likely in a relatively short time. I'll come back to add to this review some months down the road to give an update on that situation since two days of use is NOT enough time to give all the good and bad points of hardly anything.

The machine is apparently very thorough. It has five little rotary brushes that seem to do a fantastic job on carpet, and as an added bonus it's good for hard flooring as well. It appears to vacuum up its own water very well... No leaks so far except for the wastewater tank - DON'T tip it forward when carrying it to the sink (mine may have a small crack at the front, haven't investigated that yet). It appears to clean REALLY well, because damn, there's no end to the dirty water coming out of my carpets. I'm SO impressed with that, I'm just like "Riiiiiick.... why didn't you do this sooner?" It is heavy, unwieldy, and quite a workout, but that's physics, bro. If you want a good cleaning, then you need downpressure, and machines full of water are pretty much going to weigh a lot. Be a sissy, suck it up (ha ha... score a pun point!) and be clean, or have someone that isn't a sissy do it for you. In the end it is pretty rewarding. Oh and it also has a hose and a handheld attachment for doing hard to access areas like car upholstery and etc, that's a BIG bonus going for it.

Easy enough to set up and use, easy to break down and clean and store, does a nice job, cleaning solution gets used up fast so get a lot and btw it is a bit pricey. All in all, I'm happy I got it and happy I settled on this Hoover model rather than one of the many Bissel alternatives. This could quite possibly be the best $230 I've ever spent on my home and "responsible" personal life. :-)

As for dear, sweet, innocent Desi.... Within this fuzzy lump of cute lays the heart of a vengeful, destructive demon. Beware, no carpet is safe!

[...]

Update, 2-14-14: Valentine's Day and still in love with my machine, almost a year after buying her. Still running strong, and as effective as ever, though I have noticed a couple of drip-leaks along the top edge of the wastewater tank. I just hold it level when full of icky s***, and everything's fine. What I really wanted to pop in here to say is that I have a little interesting research data from recent carpet cleanings - water reclamation estimates! First, I found that to do really bad spots, the handheld hose attachment WITHOUT the rotating brush is my definite weapon of choice. Just work it hard, and it'll pull up dirt like you won't believe. By comparison, using the machine in normal mode claims a LOT less crud, so to get the same level of cleaning, you have to do it much more often.

What's really interesting is when I did the water reclamation tests today. First, I filled the cleaning solution tank with pure hot tap water (I don't use actual cleaning solutions and you'll soon see why), and then I ran the machine over a section of carpet like I usually do. While doing it, I thought to myself, "Hey, I should see how much water I claim back from the carpeting!". SO, when I was done and I took the wastewater tank to the toilet, I sat it down for a moment and made a mental note of how full it was vs. how full the cleaning solution tank still was. I cleaned out the wastewater tank, refilled the cleaning solution tank, and then poured water from the cleaning solution tank straight into the wastewater tank until it was as full as it had been after doing the carpeting. WOW, what a result. The machine was only about 50% efficient at pulling up the water out of the carpets... that's not great, but not terribly surprising.

What WAS terribly surprising was when I ran the same tests using that handheld attachment and elbow grease. AMAZING. The reclamation appears to be more like 80 - 90%! In fact, it was so good that I had a hard time seeing any loss at all. To confirm this, I ran another test - I *poured* a full 12 oz bottle of water right onto the carpet in a spiral patch and then sucked it up with the handheld attachment. Again, so high a percentile of reclaimed water I was amazed and had a hard time seeing a loss. I'll estimate about 85% until I can do better testing with a good scale to measure the actual water weights, but hell and damn am I impressed by that. Also, just in general, the cleaning job of the handheld non-rotor brush is just amazing. If you need deep cleaning, boy that's how you get it done. NICE.

Some little things to note: I did some additional reclamation testing with just the machine in normal "vacuum cleaner" style use, and now I have better methods figured out because of it. When you're working carpeting, do ONE pass on each area with the cleaning solution spraying out. Just ONE, and then several passes without it spraying to pick the water back up, moving rapidly backward and SLOWLY forward because forward motion is where you get all your pulling power. Because the reclamation isn't the best, any more water applied and you're just making a puddle down at the base of the floor that will not get pulled back up, and much of the ick goes down there with it to stay. Spray light, and the water won't be able to penetrate too deeply, and your reclamation will go way up as will your cleaning efficiency. If you really need to go deep down, then use the handheld, DO NOT use the machine in vacuum cleaner mode! Better yet, soak the holy CRAP out of the spot and then use the handheld, and it should really impress you with a great deep cleaning. Save the vacuum cleaner mode for large area, more superficial type cleaning.

Minor update, 7-18-13: The machine works as well as ever, and I continue to wage war on my evil yet deceptively cute and fluffy cat (he will use any means to convince me that he is innocent at heart!). Every so often, say a month or two apart, I haul out the machine and go to town on a swath of carpet, and I am still rewarded with filthy waste water. Here's the most interesting part about that - I long ago stopped using any kind of cleaning solution. Yep, I just use straight water now. It's WAY cheaper, obviously, and with the filthyness of the waste water I certainly can't claim that it isn't still cleaning. One of the other reasons I went to straight water is to avoid leaving any residue in the carpeting, after reading a lot about such issues on the 'Net. So there you have that. One last thing before I go for another long while - DO NOT GET LAZY AND LEAVE THE WASTE TANK FULL OF WATER! The screws that hold it all together are unprotected steel, and they will rust like freaking crazy! All four or so of the screws in the hatch part of my tank are now badly rusted because I left the machine sitting with water in the waste tank. This is not a design flaw, really, though I'm sure they could have used a better screw material had they wanted to. Rather, it's MY fault for getting lazy. Lazy has NO place in the use of this machine!

Rick NR417
top_rant.title: One Of My Best "Responsible" Decisions Ever

top_rant.text:
09-2017 ~ 05-14-2019

The printer now prints perfectly, but my printer is no longer an AnyCubic printer as I had to hack it into a new shape to cause it to print properly. These kit type printers are definately not for beginners.

FYI....
I did not purchase my AnyCubic from Amazon, if I had done so perhaps the customer-service experience would have been better? I bought the printer from an AnyCubic vendor thru eBay, in Feb 2017, it is now finally printing, May 2019.

Model sold as: AnyCubic Kossel Linear-Guide Plus for $235, Sept 2017. It was offered with no auto-bed leveling. All the glowing reviews said it was easy to configure with pre-configured settings and easy to calibrate.

Over the course of several years, I have been forced to have replaced EVERYTHING on my AnyCubic delta printer, everything that is except the long slotted 2020 profiles, the belts, pulleys, linear rails and the linear shuttles that ride on the rails, with suitable modification to the shuttles for the replaced delta-rods.

As the injected molded vertex corners were flexing I replaced those with aluminum vertex corners ($70) from UltiBots.

The printer I bought was supposed to have a heated bed, but arrived with only with a round borosilicate 'glass' bed and no bed heater. I could not cause anything to adhere to the AnyCubic bed surface overlay. I replaced all of that using a third party aluminum MK3, $31, bed heater and eventually a 3rd party PEI bed-surface for $18, (w 3M adhesive backing) which works very well, especially since it is now heated. Because the power-supply was too small to support no bed heater, I had to discard it and buy another more powerfull 360Watt power-supply ($45) from eBay and the re-wire the power cable circuit for the required increased current capacity.

The TriGorilla controller board after about a week of working with it caught fire and burned, as one of the inner PCB power-traces swolled-up shorting to ground handling the increased power, but it was rated to handle that current, apparently they fudged the specs. As it was still under warranty I tried to contact the eBay AnyCubic representative, but they were non-responsive, so I tried repairing the board myself, where upon I discovered the inner trace short and the fried MOSFET power-transistor. I chucked the board out.

Another irritation that torques me regarding AnyCubic, --although electrically the board is a CLONE of the arduino megaboard but has a different mechanical layout, and as the Arduino is offered under an OpenHardware license, AnyCubic violates that license by not providing schematics. They copy but won't don't allow other's to plagerize them, very cheesy behavior.

I replaced the board with the excellent DUET3D ARM processor based (very powerfull CPU) UK EtherNet/WiFi connected controller from FilaStruder ($180 + 10inch color TFT TouchPanel LCD - $89). Lots kewl features including not requiring constant recompiling of the firmware code to make configuration modifications, for tuning and tweaking. Instead the DUET3D utilizes direct text based G-Code configuration instructions which is stored on an SD card, while one directly controls/edits via one's computer, or via the InterNet or cellphone, etc. This provides a modern, adult interface. Unlike, AnyCubic, the DUET3D folks provides a healthy, functional user forum for tech support, and which is frequented by the actual designers of the software and hardware. There are hundreds of satisfied happy users.

The AnyCubic stepper motor cables as shipped from AnyCubic were too phukn short for usage with their own board, I was forced to modify the wires to lengthen them to be able to reach the controller.

The end-of-travel, end-stop micro-switches do not reliably switch at the same distance each time, so I replaced those with optical interrupter end-stops (3x for $20). As I originally assembling the AC printer kit, I rewired the end-stop cables with SpectraStrip flat rainbow cable, having 3 wires instead of just two so that at some future time I could replace the microswitches with optical sensors. However switch cable wires are bent over the sharp edges of the vertical profiles in a vibrational environent, which eventually will abrassively wear through the PVC insulation and will very likely create an intermittent shorts, so covered those edges with Kapton tape. It is truely a cheap printer.

The AnyCubic 'hot-end' effector also has engineering problems, about which I won't go into significant detail, but one of which is quite irksome, was the constant filament jamming, whereby one is forced to completely disassemble the hot-end to unjam the nozzle, which reveals the value of incorporating easy to disconnect hot-end connectors, which was not supplied by AnyCubic. One other is the hot-end's size and weight...its mass causes vertical fine artifacts (VFA) in the prints, that is, when one is able to actually cause the printer to print something. I replaced the entire hot-end effector with a DUET3D E3D SmartEffector, also from FilaStruder ($77) and the E3D-V6 hot-end, silicon socks, 12Vdc fan and Bowden couplers from PrintedSolid ($87). The SmartEffector has an in-built auto-leveler which works perfectly every time along with three bright white underside 'ring' mounted print area LED illuminators and hot-end heating indicator! The SmartEffector in engineering speak is sexy!

The AnyCubic diagonal rods were significantly different lengths which prevented the printer from being calibratable, and I expended many weeks trying fix that problem. In the end, I replaced the diagnonal rods and rod-end bearings using the excellent precision hand-crafted Hayden 288mm Delrin diagonal rods with mag-ball bearings both of which were supplied by UltiBots for $75. Can't get much better than that. The mag-ball bearings exhibit zero back-lash!

The AnyCubic delta bed-leveling adjustments is a nightmare, couple that with the improperly designed bed-mount bottom step being too long. When one attempts to tighten the mounts to the horizontal profile, the step is mashed into the bottom of the profile slot, which leaves a small space between the mount and the bed, in other words no matter how tight one tightens the screw one is unable to eliminate that gap and the bed can move around with a snap-noise!!! When you hear it, you'll know what I'm talking about. I used a file to grind off the bottom of the mount step about 1/8th of an inch (0.125"/3mm) which in turn enables proper tightening, but in any case the mounting system is **VERY** poorly designed as it isn't adjustable! Fortunately the DUET3D SmartEffector auto-leveler strategy creates a height-map and the software auto-flattens the bed height, but it doesn't actually resolve a tilted or mishapen bed.

The AnyCubic 'eye-bolts' as the Chinese have named them, were invented by a German fellow nameed "Heim" and are interchangeably known throughout the mechanical engineering world as either "Heim-bearings" or "Rod-End Bearings". The supplied miniature rod-end bearings are outright crap, they are different lengths, exhibit significant motion sloppines and back-lash. They must be replaced which I did using the mag-ball bearings.

Before all of those expensive modifications I wrote AnyCubic corporate offices directly in China as the eBay AnyCubic vendor did not reply. I asked for trouble-shooting help and I multiply did so over several months, eventually, I received one e-mail, (which I saved), that basically stated that they have "upgraded" their delta printer, and that I should buy a new one to fix all of the problems! Trump is correct about them. When I originally researched their product, they had posted a YouTube configuration tutorial. When I purchased the printer, which was quite fun to assemble, I really enjoyed fabricating the kit and the kit was well laid out, but that is where the 'fun' stopped as I could not cause the printer as supplied to work!! When I looked for the tutorial, I found in its place was a notice by AnyCubic stating that they were upgrading the tutorial and to check back in a few months. That tutorial did not reappear over a six month period.

The last peice which I will replace is the filament mover, the so-called 'extruder'. One can not reliably or easily guide through the extruder a 1.75mm filament without having to remove the top Bowden tube connector. Also the extruder occasionally skips. Eventually I'll replace the stepper motors with finer resolution 0.9º/step motors and the top toothless pulleys, as those are causing printing artifacts. At that juncture the only remaining components will be the 2020 profiles and linear rails, which are also very poor quality. If I could find a reasonable inexpensive replacement I would replace those as well. I had to modify the rail-shuttles in order to mount the SmartEffector diagonal-rods which then caused the belts to be offset about 12mm or 1/2inch. That's another problem which I still have to resolve.

I understand that many people are successfully printing using the standard, unmodified upgraded version of the AnyCubic delta printer, but mine was obviously not ready for production. I believe that AnyCubic sold me an early pre-production prototype for which they did not offer any customer support. They outright misrepresented the product, along with no customer support; I feel that they screwed me. So, that is the reason for this negative review. I have many photos, to prove what I've said, should anyone be interested, I'll take the time to upload them for your perusal.

HW/SW Design Engineer
top_rant.title: Can We Trust Any Glowing Reviews?

top_rant.text:
I’ve been itching to get into 3D printing for some time now but had never taken the plunge previously due to the high cost and high maintenance required to keep a 3D printer in spec.

That said, my purchase of the Makerbot Replicator 2 was an impulse buy. I just got a wild hair up mabutt and clicked the “Add to Cart”, then removed it, then added it back several times during the day. Once Amazon’s site displayed the message, “Order in the next 6 seconds and get it by Friday 14 March 2014”, I went ahead and purchased it because at that point, it was less a desire for a 3D Printer and more of a battle to see if I could add it to my cart and check out in 6 seconds. (I succeeded, BTW. Curse you Jeff Bezos for making it so easy!)

Fast forward to Friday the 14th of March when it arrived.

Unboxing:

The unit arrived in a well-packed cardboard box. The first thing one sees when opening the box is the Owner’s manual. It sits in a recessed cut-out in a large cardboard piece that sits on top of everything else in the box. Removing that cardboard “layer” exposes the 3D Printer itself. There was plenty of protection for the unit, and its internal parts (the rods and belts and gears) were secured via plastic, snap-on bars and several large, thick zip ties.

Assembly & Setup:

Since the first thing you see when opening the box is the owner’s manual I got the distinct impression the manufacturer wanted me to actually read it prior to assembly and setup. How dare they insult my geekiness! (I did follow the instructions because breaking a $2500 printer trumps gaining a few geek points) Assembly of the unit was painless and straightforward. The manual covered just about every step of the process with the only exception being “when” to connect the USB cable from the printer to the computer. (This is, btw, just an option…you do not “need” to ever connect it to a computer)

Note on PLA vs. ABS Plastic

PLA plastic is a biodegradable plastic filament made from corn or some crap like that. Printing with PLA plastic is a bit easier than ABS since its melt behavior is a bit more predictable, or so I gather from reading on various sites. It is also stronger than ABS Plastic but there is a drawback... it’s more brittle than ABS Plastic. When ABS parts are repeatedly "bent", they flex back and forth until they eventually fail. PLA Plastic will snap rather than bend and eventually fail. I’ve heard it said by people with more sensitive noses than mine that PLA Plastic, when printing, smells faintly like pancakes. ABS plastic smells like, well, nasty, old burning plastic. We all know that smell so if I’m going to spend 5+hours printing out some complex part I’d rather smell flapjacks than some toxic, puke-inducing ABS smell any day…

Initial Power Up:

Powering on the unit for the first time runs what I call the “First Run Start-Up Script”. It walks you through the process of leveling the build plate (where the printed items sit) and feeding in the filament to the extruder head, and printing a test piece.

Leveling the Build Plate:

Leveling the build plate is the single most important part of owning a 3D Printer. If you screw this part up, you’re going to have a bad day, mmkay? Go back and read the previous sentence again. Twice. Leveling the build plate ensures the extruder head prints the PLA Plastic in just the right amount. If the build plate is too close to the extruder, the PLA plastic cannot be extruded. If it’s too far away, the printed plastic is too “stringy” and does not adhere to the layer underneath. There is also another reason it is so important to level the build plate. The build plate is created from Acrylic. Even though it is relatively thick it can, and will, warp over time from being heated in one area while remaining cold in others. Some may see this cost saving step as a detriment, but I do not. It’s simply the laws of economics. Some corners had to be cut to keep the cost affordable. This was one of those cut corners. There are glass build plates available for ~$99-$109 depending on where you buy it and I do recommend purchasing one if you’re going to put the printer through heavy use cycles. I also recommend using the mfg.-supplied blue painter’s tape on the build plate. It makes removing parts easier and protects the surface of the build plate. I also recommend purchasing about 20 of these strips (less than $30 at the mfg.’s site)

Printing your first piece:

Printing one of the designs from the included SD card the printer ships with is probably the best way to see if the printer is properly setup and operational. I recommend the forever bracelet and the nut & bolt. Both put the printer through its paces and provide you with an idea what they are capable of doing.

Mfg-supplied Software:

The software is not included. You must go to the mfg site and download the software. It is basic print-only software. It does not allow you to “create” or “edit” files. You can print it or save it in a different format.

3D CAD Software:

There are many different kinds of free 3D CAD software out there so I will not cover all of them. I haven’t touched a CAD program since 1990 so I am approaching this as someone who is brand new to CAD and just wants to get started making things, rather than getting an engineering degree before I even power the printer up. I will cover only the ones I tried, and I my “rating” of them is, at best, subjective and based on my personal learning curve…YMMV, obviously, depending on your experiences with CAD software.

1 – Blender (Freeware)
Pro: Very powerful! Tons and tons of options and capabilities!
Con: Overly complicated for the “Novice” user, in my opinion. To me, it was a PITA compared to the other CAD programs, TBH. I could print everything I ever wanted to print using the other programs below before I could master this software enough to design & print 1 item.

2 – 123Make/Design (Free online version. Design stuff right in your browser!)
Pro: Surprisingly easy to get started with and create stuff quickly.
Con: It sometimes doesn’t want to let you download what you created. It’s not so much a case where it wants you to upgrade to the premium account…the wwb site just has some "eccentric" idiosyncrasies you must deal with, though they are tolerable enough that I often use this program.

3 -- TinkerCAD(Free online version. Design stuff right in your browser!)
Pro: Also surprisingly easy to use and also able to quickly get stuff created.
Con: Not “quite” as powerful as the 123 software, though it is close enough that I use it as much as the 123 software. I use this about 50% of the time and the 123 software the other 50%. Often times, I'll create in this program, then "tweak" the design in 123 since it allows a little more fine-grain control.

CAD Software Summary:

In case you haven’t guessed by now, I use #’s 2 & 3 most often. When I’m done with what I’m designing, I save/download the file as a .stl file and import that into the Makerbot print software. I could also copy the .stl file to the SD card and put it into the SD Card reader on the printer and print out the part without using the computer at all. (In case I want to get some gaming in while watching my print)

Failed Prints:

I have had 2 prints fail. In each case, however, the failure was my design, not a defect in the printer.

Failure 1 – In the first instance, I started printing a part and realized that, because of my poor design, there would be an un-supported section that would most likely fail, so I stopped the print. (Think of “printing” a wall with a doorway…if you printed it lying down, as a flat surface, it’d look like a flat square with a missing section, where the doorway is located. If you print it oriented vertically, the “arch” of the doorway is not supported and the plastic would dip down and ruin the print, so I stopped the print process about 1 minute into the print so I could design in support for the archway of the door)

Failure 2 – In the second failure, it was due to my forgetfulness. I have an Ultra USB 3.0 USB to SATA Adapter so I created a USB 3.0 Docking Station to put it in so I can hot swap my spare SSDs. In the design phase, I realized it needed an internal width of 1mm larger than I initially drew it... When I went back into the program to widen it, I got sidetracked with adding some bells and whistles to the design and completely forgot to widen it, lol.

Sources of 3D Objects:

Thingiverse is a major repository of 3D Designs you can access and download to print or modify. There are thousands and thousands of designs available and you may find what you want to print has already been created, thereby saving you much time and effort. Additionally, there are many conversion programs available that convert one file type into another so even if the item you want is in a different format, you may be able to export or convert it to a language the Replicator 2 can print

My impressions after having used it for four days:

I’m happy with the purchase thus far. And, I’m also cognizant of the fact these printers require maintenance and replacement parts on a fairly regular basis…which increases their cost of ownership. It’s something you should keep in mind as well unless you have $2500 to just throw away.

I’m having a lot of fun designing and making things, and I’m learning CAD skills as I go along. The more stuff I make, the more I think, “Gee, I want to make one of those XXXX too!!!”.

I started with only the goal of printing some fan grills, case parts, and air ducts for my computer. My list has grown to 60+ items I now want to make, and the list keeps growing and growing and growing…This is a good sign because it means I plan on using the heck out of this printer…and I’d better be using the heck out of this printer for the price I paid…
top_rant.title: My Mini-Review

top_rant.text:
Disclosure: I bought my printer direct from PrintrBot because I wanted a red one and you can't get red from Amazon. This isn't my first 3D printer, it's my second, my first was is SeeMeCNC Rostock Max [great printer BTW] that I built from a kit a year and a half ago (and I’ll be doing a lot of comparisons to it through out this review [which really shouldn’t be fair to the Play, as the SeeMeCNC Rostock Max is best in class for Delta 3D printers]). I was a little distrusting therefore to have a printer preassembled (though you can't get red any other way, and I wanted Proto Type Red TM, as I am building prototypes after all). However, I will admit, PrintrBot did it right providing a test cube proving that it did in fact work correctly when it left the PBHQ (as they like to call it), and in fact when I fed my grey PLA into it it pushed out some green left over from the test print, very good indeed! I'm impressed with the attention to detail and the concern to make sure they are shipping a finished product, more on that later.

Unboxing impressions: Simply amazing! The box is a brown EOM style box, but the packing is first rate. Everything comes out in an organized manor where you encounter items in the order in which you should. In fact the first thing that greeted me was that PrintrBot was going to treat me to a free year of AutoDesk Fusion 360, provided on a beautiful card cut from and into an aluminium plate. Autodesk typically offers Fusion 360 as a free month trial, and then $40 per month after that, or $300 a year. So a free year trial is worth 75% of the price of the printer itself, what a bonus! (Now I don't know if the trial is included with all of the printers, or if that is something specially because I purchased the "special edition" red one.) Over all though, there is some serious attention to detail with the entire unboxing experience, and you can see PrintrBot is really focus on the user experience, and that can be seen at every step in the process.

Printer Design: This printer is the first printer at this price point I’ve seen that truly appears to be “finished”. This is quite remarkable really. The difference between this printer and the “simple maker’s kit” (which it replaces) I would liken to the difference between the Apple I (a motherboard that still needed a power supply and all other IO components) and the Apple II which was a computer by today’s standards! PrintrBot has done an amazing job of reducing part count and combining assemblies. This has the net effect of producing a much more rigid, accurate, reliable, and trouble free printer. By this standard the Play may well be the best designed printer on the market today, including printers multiple times the expense.

One con that should go under design is the lack of a detachable bed. A detachable bed is SUPER great! It makes getting pieces off easier (especially when you slap it in the freezer they just pop right off). Its makes bed preparation easier because the printer isn’t in the way. It makes it easy to just switch out the bed when one print is done and you can just start printing the next! Hopefully there will be an upgrade for this. One half star off for this oversight.

Hot End: All metal Ubis hot end!!! This is amazing! I just got done spending almost $100 to add an E3D v6 hot end to my Rostock Max (which is a $1000 printer unassembled), and this little guy comes with a hot end just as good out of the box! That’s 25% the value of the cost of the whole unit! Of course, this only makes the lack of a heated bed that much more depressing, as you can’t simply jump right in to all of the awesome opportunities, but still! Also, it has a .4 nozzle diameter, the same as the E3D v6 stock.

Extruder: All metal extruder! The quality just keeps coming. …what was the price of this thing again? SeeMeCNC’s “EZStruder”, a fine extruder, seriously love it, is made of mostly plastic parts and cost $35! And SeeMeCNC was setting a new bar for value when they released that.

PrintrBot made the right decision going with 1.75mm filament. 1.75mm is the new standard, 3mm will probably eventually go away, or at least be really hard to find, and it makes metering a lot easier.

Also because this is a direct drive and not a Bowden extruder it will make jams and such much less likely, and easier to fix when they do happen. Though honestly I haven’t had much issue with the Bowden on my Rostock, but it is nice not having the fiddle with getting the filament down the tube or back out again when switching filaments.

Print volume: Some people have marked the play down because of its small print volume. This really isn’t fair. It’s like marking down a motorcycle because it doesn’t have the transport ability of a passenger car. If you want a passenger car, buy one. However, the 4”x4”x5” print volume is a very useful volume. It may not be big enough for some projects to print in a single piece, but many projects are assemblies anyway, and it’s a limitation that is easily over come (and likely saves your bacon, err, I mean plastic).

The fact of the matter is the larger you make your print the more opportunity there is for things to go wrong, period. It is really hard to get big prints to actually work out. The longer the run of plastic on a print bed, the more likely it is to come off the bed during the print! The tall the print, the more likely you are to have inner layer lamination issues (because of variations in the temps of the layers, and the more layers the more variation)! The taller the print, the more leverage forces that are applied to the part and thus causes additional issues, including detaching from the bed. The bigger the print, the longer it takes, and just more time for something to happen, power to go out (get a UPS, seriously, just do it), cat/child/spouse to get in the room, a stepper motor to skip a step (oh it’s nice to come back and see the printer printing in thin air), etc. …see the trend here?

I’m speaking from personal experience on this. Small prints are easier prints, period! The play is aimed at entry-level users, so having a larger print area invites you to get into trouble. Additionally, as you increase the print volume of your part your print time goes up exponentially (and I mean that, exponentially), you can easy go from something that takes an hour or two to something that takes multiple days. So at first I was all about printing large models, because I was like, “oh look, how cool is that!” But in honesty, I hate large prints, they take days to complete, there are loads of things that can go wrong (and do), and it’s a great way to use up a lot of plastic and time and have nothing to show for it. Also, once you stop using the printer as a toy, and you start using it to build your own designs, you’re probably not going to need that much space.

Now, some of you will probably think, “why is he trying to talk me out of wanting a large print volume?” I’m not. There’s nothing wrong with it, you just need to know what you are getting into, and understand what this product is, and how a large print volume is not in line with it’s design goals (to make a 3D printer as approachable as possible for the newbie, thus why it’s called the “play” and not the “madness”). There are times I want to make a large print, but those times don’t come up nearly as often as you’d think, and I think LONG and HARD about it before I go down that road, and I always ask myself, “can I do this differently?”

Audience: Everybody! From the last section you might have gotten the idea that I was trying to say that this is a tool (or a toy depending on how you wish to view it) for the newbie. And though the printer is obviously very conscious of the 3D printing neophyte, I believe this is an awesome value proposition for the 3D printing guru as well! If you don’t do much 3D printing at $399 this is a great printer, and the print volume will almost certainly suffice, and you aren’t out that much money. If you do “do” a lot of printing, you will quickly realize that 3D printing is SLOW! You don’t want to double up on your prints, because that only increases your chances of something going wrong, wrecking all of your parts, and wasting time and plastic (and it’s not really any faster anyway, because you still have the same total number of head movements). The best way to solve this is with more than one printer. At $399 you could buy two Plays for the cost of a single Rostock Max (which will come unassembled and require about 40 hours to build your first time) and have $200 left over, or three Plays for the cost of a PrintrBot Plus, or five Plays for the cost of a MakerBot Replicator 2, etc. See where I’m going with this? This is a GREAT machine to have around to print your small parts (which will be most of them), and you can afford to double, triple, quadruple, . . . , up for the price, there by allowing you to keep working while your large print volume printer is making that three day print!

This is truly a great printer, either to increase your printer count, or to buy to get into 3D printing to start with (and then later when you want a bigger printer you still have cash left over and you have a second on hand!). Besides, you might actually be better off having multiple Plays printing pieces of a larger part and then gluing it together, it will get done faster, it will be less likely to fail, and if one does fail you haven’t lost everything. Also, it’s nice to have more than one printer, because if something breaks on one, you can use the other to fix it!

One con that should go under the audience heading, and it’s the only “serious” con I’ve encountered is that they opted for a z-height probe instead of a heated bed. A heated bed is the single biggest up grade for improving a plastic adhering to the bed, and it doesn’t really matter what plastic you are using, everything sticks better to a heated bed. Whereas even though bed leveling is important, and can be difficult and you have to get that right to have good prints, the reality is that you can use software to make the bed leveling pretty darn easy (looking at you MatterControl, you rock!), but there is no software solution to a cold bed! Having both is great, but if it’s one or the other, I’d go with the heated bed every time! Now in fairness, Brook (the CEO of PrintrBot) has stated they are planning an upgrade option, but honestly this should have been stock, and the z-probe should have been the upgrade option. So I have taken a star off for this.

Conclusion: This is the most remarkable follow up product I have seen in a VERY long time. The Play is truly a MASSIVE step forward over the previous entry level PrintrBots, and represents a groundbreaking advancement in the cost/value proposition in desktop 3D printing in the industry as a whole. That is quite an accomplishment (for which the Play earns back the half star it lost for not having the removable bed). It is truly disappointing that the Play does not come with a heated bed for all of the otherwise awesome there is in this truly extraordinary product, but that is a problem you can either hack or wait for the official solution to, but in the end, there is absolutely no reason for everyone who is into 3D printing to not own -at least- one Play … yes, it’s that good.
top_rant.title: Buy one, either as your first or your fifteenth!

top_rant.text:
======== UPDATED == June 19, 2015 =========
======== Update
I have REALLY put this thing through its paces now. I have printed many, MANY, product design ideas of mine. I wish I could show you, but most of this stuff is patentable. It is SO AWESOME to see ideas I have had sitting in my notebooks for over a decade finally come to life! I have got to say that I am blown away by this printer.
I have had only two problems out all the hundreds of parts I have printed so far (since the review below that is). One was that one of the prints I did, everything turned out 'bumpy'...but I think the software (or my PC) was misbehavin' and a reboot solved that...not happened in nearly 100 prints since then.
The only actual 'failed' print I EVER had was with a tall thin part (like 0.040" thin) that I carelessly printed on a bed with 3 layers on old hairspray I hadn't cleaned off the bed at the end of a day of 12 hrs of printing. It knocked loose half way thru the 3" tall part. My fault really. Cleaned the bed with a wet cloth...dried it...re-hairspraied it and printed perfectly. At least the other parts I printed during the failed print turned out fine...just had to clean off the birds nest.
I will say that if I could give this thing 6 stars at this point, I would....exceeded all my expectations in 3D printing in general. I am so happy I decided to keep the thing after the problems I had (as I discuss below in the original review).
You should note that I have added several new videos to the Robo3D section of my YT channel on basic printer setup, tips and tricks on use and so forth. I hope you find this information useful, read the review below to find out where to see them.
I am really surprised that I have had so few problems with this printer, especially after my initial nightmare with it! Going into 3D printing, I couldn't have imagined a device capable of running SO MANY HOURS without a real problem. You think about buying an HP laser printer and how few hours you ACTUALLY run THAT before it breaks! I have gone through several of those in my time.
I know my saying that I have substantially had only one failed print (since the repair talked about below) may sound like a load of complete BS to you, but I SWEAR it is true....that is partly due to just good 3D print-ready part design practices as well as a good printer....and I couldn't be more surprised as that is NOT AT ALL what I thought I was in for with these new technologies at this stage in the industry. I mean, these things were all made of friggin plywood just a couple of years ago! Robo3D does deserve credit for a simple but reliable printer in the end, even if you have to sometimes jump through a hoop or two to get it going at first!
By the way, I have NEVER had to manually level the bed to this day. That process alone (compared to other printers out there) has saved me COUNTLESS hours of hassle. Also of interest is the fact that several of the prints I have done really used the entire print footprint...that 9" x 10" area with the 8" ceiling came in handy on several parts I did.
The only thing that started to sound like 'wear' on the Robo3D was it started making a 'droning' sound several dozen prints ago. I just grabbed some 3 in 1 oil and lubed (with my finger) the X-axis rods and (without actually removing the glass bed which I have yet to do....even though it is attached by magnets), lubed the part of the Y-axis I could reach with my finger, and just let the motion of the printer lube the bearing block as it prints. Problem solved....no more 'droning' noise.
Anyway, I hope you find my review below useful as many people have...and I really appreciate your time reading it as well as those who have found the review helpful in the past.
======== End Update

PREFACE ==================================
I would probably give this more like 4.5 stars if I could. I am not going to say I had a great time with this printer at first as it was a bit of a rocky experience. My rating of it is based on the PRODUCT ITSELF for the most part... but I will comment on the experience, company and customer service as well.

Firstly, you will note that I AM an Amazon VERIFIED PURCHASER (despite a good deal or reluctance given the reviews at the time of purchase which was November 10, 2014). I am finally writing reviews for many of the purchases I have made over the last year. I haven't tried to do any sort of product reviews for WELL OVER A DECADE due to my attempt to review something on Amazon back in 2000-2001 and having a hard time doing so... and I think that in the end I couldn't post the review for some reason if I remember correctly...so I have more or less stayed out of social-anything since then (not enough time and too much of a pain in the ass).
Also, I like to have some experience using a product before I would consider reviewing it plus I hadn't had time to look into how to do it (as a verified purchaser) before now so I haven't bothered with reviews for the most part in the past year of purchases (but I resolved to finally do reviews on them at some point this year since these types of reviews really have helped me on my purchase decisions SO GREATLY). Additionally, given the effort I have put into 3D printer research since the middle of 2013 and the fact that the reviews on THIS product have caused some tribulation on my part, I was motivated to finally figure it out.
Note that I have looked at many printers over the last year+ including Makerbot, Makergear, Isis3D (dont ask), Robo3D, DaVinci, EZ3D, g-Max, Type Amachines, Fusion3Ddesigns, Solidoodle, Airwolf, Cubify (GREEDY filament RIP OFF@!), Plastic Scribbler, ORD Solutions, FlashForge, Ultimaker, Taz, Afinia and Rigidbot to name JUST a few.

Secondly, I am in Tulsa, Oklahoma and I will say that I have NO AFFILIATION with Robo3D and have never met these guys in any way nor do I have any vested interest in writing them a good review. I DO BELIEVE they have, at the very least, the makings of a good product here and that deserves a fair review.

Thirdly, the print dimensions of the printer are wrong on Amazon I believe, it has a print volume of 10"x 9" (length and width), with a height of 8".

The REVIEW =============================================
Before I go any further, I suggest you read this review before checking out my YouTube video (link later in review). The review you are reading is not generally repeated in the video (and the video is 45 minutes long) so this is a quicker review than the video.

Now, unfortunately the Amazon review below titled "Move along, sir."By "Das" on October 16, 2014 was prophetic to my experience as far as the fan and leaking extruder were concerned...even though that was not a verified purchaser. I had come to understand that the problem had been fixed, so I decided to go ahead with the purchase.

THE REASON I TOOK THE RISK -----------
This is due to the fact that the specifications were superior to the only real alternative in my budget range. The alternative was XYZprinting's DaVinci. Robo3D has superior build volume (even to Makergear's M2 I believe); has no chipped cartridges (like ANY products from friggin Cubify or the DaVinci whose cartridges supposedly develop tangles in the filament and will also fall apart); they wont brick your machine if you try to use other software or hacks like XYZprinting supposedly will; you don't have to calibrate it all the time; and it can use a LARGE array of other filament types and filament from other manufacturers (again no chipped cartridges). For example, I am using a $20 one kg spool of red filament from Hatchbox I bought on Amazon and I have used most of the spool with NOT A SINGLE PROBLEM.
But I knew there were risks, however I figured for the $800 price, if I had problems with it, I could return it to Amazon and (again for the same price) get a DaVinci AND several spools of chipped filament. I figured that I will probably buy another printer next year when I have a better budget so the filament I purchased for the Robo3D could be used on the NEXT 3d printer (so it wasn't a waste of money to purchase several spools of PLA/ABS for the Robo3D).
This was the first 3D printer I have actually had my hands on (or seen in action in person for that matter), and after over 350 hours of research on 3D printers since the middle of 2013, and for the price, I was AT LEAST looking for a starter printer to get some "hands on" experience. I figured that even if I sent the Robo3D back, I at least would get experience with this printer before exchanging it for another (ie DaVinci), thus giving me a frame of reference for the DaVinci at least.
In the end, I was FULLY COMMITTED to sending the damn thing back if I had a problem of ANY KIND when I made the Robo3D purchase.

BUT HANG ON.... IT ACTUALLY WORKS! -----------
HOWEVER, when I actually unboxed the thing and set it up following their video, it actually WORKED! NO CALIBRATION, No hassles, NOTHING. From ALL the research on 3D printers I had done over the last year+, I was sure this was going to be at least a little bit of a pain in the butt (like the reviews on the DaVinci indicate for example)... but it actually worked as advertised.
BUT, I quickly noticed the sick-sounding fan and said "that is a bad sign, I had better stress test this to see if I have a leaky extruder", and sure enough, I did (see video for detailed history of this).
But in the process, I liked the thing so much that I decided to give Robo3D a shot at setting this right. I wouldn't have done this unless this knocked my socks off since I think it is ridiculous that I was having to repair a product I had owned for a week! I HATE THINGS that are a pain in the ass to boot... but I fully realized that this will have to be done with likely ANY printer eventually, and at least the Robo3D is open source, so even if they go out of business for some reason chances are I will find some way to replace parts if that were an issue. Contrast this to DaVinci which with the lawsuits they are likely to get from bricking their printers, they MIGHT go out of business, and they ARENT open source.

TIME TO FIX THE THING -----------
In any event, I contacted Robo3D via e-mail, they sent me a repair kit with no instructions as far as I know (but their old printer kit videos from their Kickstarter campaign in 2013 were helpful) and I set out to not only repair the thing, but make YouTube videos of how to do it. I will release these videos (in addition to the one which is currently on YouTube) in the next few days after editing.
It was a little bit tough to get the screws which retain the extruder out, but it wasn't a big deal really to do the repair. And again, with the state of the industry at the moment you can expect to have to do things like this with any printer at some point.
Now the printer is printing leak-free. So when they told me that they had a few bad units floating around on shelves out there at the time I purchased the printer, but the problem is fixed now (ie new fan mfgr and properly tightened extruder), I believe them fully because IT WORKS. See my video to see the results of the 'post-repair' print stress tests I have ran. I have printed far more objects than I had before the repairs, and the extruder is CLEAN AS CAN BE...no leaks, and the fan is far less annoying than before!
Just being able to un-box the thing and print with no calibration and default software settings was a great first 3D printer experience for me! I haven't calibrated it or leveled the bed ONCE since I purchased it...it self-levels.
Despite the necessity for the repair work on it, I believe the printer was well worth it!

SOFTWARE -----------
As far as their software is concerned, yea it has some bugs (open source)... but it will get the basic job done if you are new to 3d printing. But given that it is open source, you could use other programs instead like Cura and Simplify 3d, etc...but for just getting started as a newbie, the software is adequate.
In fact the whole process was simple enough that even someone who is just decently computer literate could set up and use the thing to print objects off Thingoverse or any other .stl file.

CUSTOMER SERVICE -----------
UPDATE *************************************
All the reviews I have seen in the last couple of month have given their customer service glowing praise. So they probably have the wrinkles worked out on that issue.
END UPDATE *************************************
Regarding their customer service, yea it could be faster, but it is NOT NON-EXISTANT! They were quite helpful to me in getting this done (even called me on the phone), so it isn't fair to say it isn't there. But this is a new and growing company in what is shaping up to be a nescient but very competitive industry and I can tell you from experience as a former technology manufacturer that they are running around like chickens with their heads cut off.
A million details to take care of on top of product bugs (inevitable for a new and somewhat complex product from a new company to boot) and trying to break into retail (a VITAL necessity at this stage) are what they are going through...and BELIEVE ME, all that isn't easy... but they have actually done a fairly good job on their product with all things considered.
Keep in mind that the competition is heating up and they know it. The first couple of years of business in this would break most people, but they have kept going and I think they deserve to have some tolerance given what they have achieved. They REALLY do have a good product here and one that I believe is suitable for a newbie to this area.

WHY I CARE -----------
Given my own experience in better days as a new company and manufacturer in the mid '90's in the booming field of LED lighting technology targeted at signage (the real origin of the stuff), I feel a certain kindred relationship with these guys and the 3D printing industry in general...so it was time to figure all this social media junk out and spend some (long) hours giving them a fair review. I love this thing so I hope you might give them a fair chance (given all the bad reviews and perhaps dubious good reviews... I know, I had thought the same thing!).

MY COMPUTER PLATFORM -----------
I should note that I am running this printer from a Windows 7 PC (an old Dell Optiplex 755 Core2Duo) and have not had any problems with the unit disconnecting USB-wise or any other funny business. Also, I have it hooked to a 15' USB cable I bought on Amazon with no problems.

IN CONCLUSION -----------
I am not going to say that it is a perfect product. For example, there's no LCD screen, would be nice to at least have a 'quick remove' shroud or alternate shroud design for easy access, LED lights on print head, better software with much fewer bugs, easier hole to load filament in, etc, but I could critique ANY product that way!!! You WONT get perfection out of ANY product, some more than others I'll grant you, but NONE are perfect! However I was quite impressed overall with I actually got out of the box.
Even though it doesn't look as cool as DaVinci (which was what made it harder to buy a Robo3D instead), it is still much better done than I would have expected from something that was on Kickstarter in 2013!
In the end, I was NOT looking for a new friggen HOBBY (I simply don't have time for such things), but I WAS looking for a tool I could develop ideas with...and I do believe that the Robo3D is a decent tool to start with! And since it doesn't hurt to have more than one printer (given the speed of these things), I don't think you will go wrong with a Robo3D.
My advice, purchase it from someone you can return it to (like Amazon), and if you have a bad extruder like I did, just return it and get them to send you a new one. Hopefully the units with extruder problems are off the shelves since I purchased mine, but you could always watch my videos and repair yours as well since it isn't that difficult for those who can use simple tools and follow a video.
Now if you get a machine that doesn't print right out of the box (like for some of the reviews I have read), send it back immediately and get another one because it REALLY SHOULD print just fine right out of the box.
Given that I am new to 3d printing, I cannot comment on the relative quality as compared to a $2000+ printer, but I am not disappointed at all. You can get a sense of the quality from my YouTube video since I review every single thing I have printed so far on it.
These guys still have a ways to go, but they are getting there and their product is a good solid start if you are new to 3D printing (just follow my advice above if you have problems).
I Hope this review helps you like so many reviewers efforts have helped me over the last year.
UPDATE *************************************
At the time I am updating this review, I have had no further problems with the printer. I still have yet (thank god) to have a clogged nozzle. I thought I did once, but it turned out to be a bug in the software with their Cura engine that was just trying to print too close to the glass bed so nothing could come out….switch back to the MatterSlice engine and no clogged nozzle!
By the way, my Saintsmart filament works great also.
END UPDATE *************************************

VIDEO REVIEW -----------
My review video (and all my Robo3D videos) can be viewed on YouTube at:
(copy & paste the following link into your browser)
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLHRsI8vbnO5dXvwTAmZC28eH52syhxock

The Review Video ONLY is at the following link:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yd71B8BBUGk&feature=youtu.be

The YouTube title of my FIRST video is:
"Robo3D R1 Review / Extruder Repair Video 1"
Or search YouTube or Google for "Robert's Grip" or the title above if you are
having trouble (Don't forget the apostrophe! in 'Robert's' though)

Sorry for the long review and video, but once I get going...
